Rules about who is allowed to touch what need to be encoded in harness capabilities, risk tiers, and review routing rather than relying mainly on social norms, because agents will operate wherever their tools permit.

assumptions: Agents are already used for a large share of first-pass changes via harnesses or CLIs.
The organization can technically enforce harness-level policies and capabilities per area or team.
Stacks are at least moderately opinionated and testable so that interface and policy ownership are meaningful.
Human review and on-call remain mandatory for production systems; full unsupervised autonomy is out of scope.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Team topology should remain mostly as today, with classic service or repo ownership and on-call, and agents treated as local tools; the main adaptations should be better guidelines and training for individuals, not a structural redesign of ownership or escalation.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In small or low-maturity orgs without strong tests or harnesses, attempting this topology may add complexity and new failure modes—unclear boundaries, confused escalation, and brittle policy enforcement—without enough benefit over simpler, human-centric ownership.
verification_targets: Map one real agent-first team’s incidents and PRs to the proposed ownership and escalation model and see if it reduces ambiguity compared to current practice.
Pilot a distinct automation/harness on-call rotation and measure whether incident resolution and attribution improve for agent-caused issues.
Implement basic harness-level capability boundaries in a monolith and track whether cross-team ownership violations and review pain decrease.
open_questions: What is the lightest-weight way to encode agent boundaries and risk tiers in the harness without burdening small teams?
How do these patterns adapt to polyglot microservice environments where agents act across many repos and infra layers?
At what scale of agent usage do separate automation/harness on-call and platform teams become clearly beneficial rather than overkill?

question.text: For agent-first teams using triad pairing and a craft-maturity ladder, how could we redesign on-call rotations, incident review, and "ownership" handoffs so that the review bottleneck doubles as a structured apprenticeship surface—transferring boundary judgment and verification taste from seniors to juniors—without slowing down response or overloading the few senior custodians?

artifacts.answer:
Outline of a lightweight redesign that turns on-call and incidents into structured apprenticeship without tanking responsiveness.

1) Role-graded on-call pairs
- Tiered pairing by craft ladder:
  - Tier 3 (System Crafter): solo for core; may pair with Tier 2 on complex windows.
  - Tier 2 (Emerging): primary on less-critical areas but always paired with a Tier 3 shadow for escalations.
  - Tier 1 (Operator): shadow on-call only; handles low-risk ops under playbooks.
- Rotation rule: every Tier 1/2 on-call shift must be paired with a higher-tier partner reachable within minutes.

2) Incident triad pattern (senior + junior + agent)
- During an incident:
  - Agent: runs diffs, log/metric queries, test runs via harness.
  - Junior: drives the harness, writes the incident note, proposes the first fix.
  - Senior: decides risk class, approves high-risk actions, and narrows scope.
- Hard rule: irreversible actions (schema/data/money) require senior approval; agent can stage plans and dry-runs only.

3) Fast, structured post-incident mini-review
- Within 24–48h, 20–30 min max.
- Inputs: short timeline, diffs, harness logs.
- Prompts aimed at taste/apprenticeship:
  - Where did boundary understanding fail or help?
  - Which verification step actually caught or would have caught this?
  - What would we want a Tier 1/2 to notice next time?
- Outputs (pick 1–2 only):
  - A new or clarified runbook step.
  - A small test or contract change.
  - A harness rule or checklist tweak.
- Junior who was primary writes the final summary and PRs any test/runbook changes; senior just reviews.

4) Ownership handoffs as "boundary tours"
- When changing ownership of an area:
  - Do a 60–90 min "boundary tour" instead of a big doc.
  - Senior walks through: key flows, no-go zones, canary tests, and recent incidents.
  - Junior runs the agent to answer 1–2 real queries ("find all writes to X", "where do we call Y vendor?") and narrates what they see.
- Handoff checklist (very short):
  - Named primary + backup owners and their craft tier.
  - Key incident runbooks linked.
  - 3–5 "must-not-break" tests.

5) Making the review bottleneck the apprenticeship surface
- On-call / incident PRs and runbook changes get tier-aware routing:
  - If primary author is Tier 1 → reviewer must be Tier 3.
  - If Tier 2 → reviewer can be Tier 2 or 3, but risky changes require 3.
- Simple reviewer prompts (checkboxes or canned comments):
  - "Did the junior choose sane blast radius for the fix?"
  - "Did they add/adjust the right test or runbook step?"
  - "Is the boundary of ownership clearer after this change?"
- Use these answers as craft-ladder evidence (not just gut feel) alongside regular PRs.

6) Load management for seniors
- Cap synchronous senior involvement:
  - Clear hours/week budget per senior for live incidents and reviews.
  - Use agents + templates to offload rote work: diff summaries, log digests, draft postmortems.
- Escalation funnel:
  - Tier 1 pages their Tier 2 partner first.
  - Only Tier 2 pages Tier 3.
  - Auto-page Tier 3 only for pre-classified high-blast-radius alerts.

7) Integration with craft-maturity ladder
- Tier transitions tied to on-call + incident behavior, not just feature PRs:
  - T1→T2: handles a target number of shadow shifts and low-risk incidents where postmortems show good reasoning and boundary awareness.
  - T2→T3: leads several incidents end-to-end, proposes and lands verification or guardrail improvements that seniors endorse.
- Keep a tiny log per engineer: notable incidents, their role, and key learning/changes shipped.

Net effect: on-call, incidents, and handoffs become repeatable surfaces where juniors practice boundary judgment and verification, while agents carry execution load and seniors focus on short, high-leverage decisions instead of doing all the work themselves.

definitions.name: agent-first workflow
definitions.meaning: A development loop where the first serious draft, analysis, or implementation attempt is delegated to an agent before the human codes directly.

definitions.name: agent harness
definitions.meaning: A tool environment that lets a model act with files, shell commands, diffs, and other capabilities instead of only producing chat responses.

definitions.name: craft bar
definitions.meaning: The explicit or implicit standard a team uses for elegance, coherence, maintainability, and fit in shipped code.

definitions.name: review bottleneck
definitions.meaning: The point where human validation, not code generation, becomes the main constraint on shipping safely.

definitions.name: implementation abundance
definitions.meaning: A condition where generating plausible code is cheap relative to deciding what to build and how to verify it.

claims.text: In agent-first teams with implementation abundance, on-call and incident handling can be redesigned as tiered, triad-based workflows where juniors drive harness use and seniors gate irreversible actions, turning the review bottleneck into a primary apprenticeship surface without materially slowing response.

Short, structured post-incident reviews that focus on boundary judgment, verification steps, and small test or runbook changes can transfer senior taste to juniors more reliably than long, root-cause-heavy postmortems.

Using a craft-maturity ladder to grade on-call roles, incident responsibilities, and ownership handoffs creates a feedback loop where real production behavior drives maturation while keeping senior load bounded through pairing rules and escalation funnels.

assumptions: The team already uses an agent harness and can reliably log which tools and actions agents take during incidents.
Craft maturity tiers are roughly stable and accepted by the team as a basis for staffing and on-call decisions.
Alerting and runbooks can be tuned so that most incidents allow at least a small amount of structured human interaction rather than requiring constant heroics.
There are enough Tier 2–3 engineers to support a tiered on-call and escalation model without chronic overload.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: On-call and incident work should remain as simple and flat as possible, with minimal role differentiation; adding triads, tiers, and structured incident reviews mainly adds cognitive and process overhead, slowing response and burning senior time without significantly improving junior apprenticeship.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In small or under-staffed teams with few true seniors and frequent high-severity incidents, the proposed tiering and triad patterns may be impossible to staff; seniors become the universal escalation point anyway, skip structured reviews to keep up, and the apprenticeship benefits never materialize while everyone’s load increases.
verification_targets: Pilot tiered on-call and triad incident handling for a subset of services and measure response time, senior pages per week, and junior escalations before vs. after.
Compare incident postmortems from before and after introducing short, taste-focused mini-reviews to see if they result in more concrete test/runbook improvements driven by juniors.
Track changes in craft-maturity assessments for juniors who participate in these on-call triads versus peers who do not over a 6–12 month period.
open_questions: What is the smallest viable set of incident and on-call rituals that still produces noticeable apprenticeship gains without adding too much latency?
How should these patterns change for very high-availability, low-latency systems where even short structured discussions during incidents are hard to sustain?
Can parts of the apprenticeship signal extraction (e.g., detecting good boundary choices or verification steps) be automated from incident logs and diffs without encouraging gaming?

question.text: If we assume implementation abundance but deliberately *remove* most existing harness constraints—no boundary tags, few risk lanes, minimal verification scripts—and instead make humans responsible only for post-hoc diff review and incident handling, what breaks first in real teams (architecture, safety, craft, or pace), and how would that breakdown challenge the current belief that stronger harnesses are the primary lever for preserving judgment and taste in agent-first workflows?

artifacts.answer:
Most teams that drop harness constraints but keep agents and rely only on diff review will see safety and craft degrade first, with architecture drifting next and pace eventually stalling. This pattern weakens the idea that harness strength alone protects judgment and taste; it suggests the main levers are where the review bottleneck sits and how much context and time reviewers actually have.

Compact view:
- What breaks first:
  1) **Safety**: more subtle regressions, incident load, and fear of touching areas.
  2) **Craft**: inconsistent patterns, noisier diffs, shallow tests, copy-paste fixes.
  3) **Architecture**: slow boundary erosion that becomes visible through incidents and brittle tests.
  4) **Pace**: net speed drops as incident handling and rework eat into any generation gains.

- Why safety and craft go first:
  - Agents keep shipping plausible code; without harness checks, reviewers see large, mixed-quality diffs.
  - Humans under time pressure skim and rely on green tests; gaps in tests and verification mean more production defects.
  - Taste is hard to exercise over big, dense diffs; reviewers default to “good enough.”

- How this challenges “stronger harnesses are the main lever”:
  - The core constraint becomes **human review capacity and attention**, not just whether the harness is strict.
  - Even strong harnesses fail if reviewers are overloaded or context-poor; conversely, small, high-context teams with weak harnesses but very selective merging can preserve taste better than expected.
  - The most important design choice is **where to place constraints relative to the review bottleneck**:
    - At task intake (what work is agent-eligible).
    - At diff granularity (enforcing small, scoped changes).
    - At human merge gates (who can approve which areas, with what tests).

- Implication for agent-first workflows:
  - Harness rules help, but they are supporting tools for human judgment, not substitutes.
  - To preserve taste and safety under implementation abundance, teams should:
    - Keep diffs small and boundary-scoped.
    - Route high-risk changes to high-context reviewers.
    - Use harness checks mainly to keep reviewable units clean, not to be the only guardians of craft.

claims.text: In implementation-abundant teams that remove most harness constraints and rely mainly on post-hoc diff review, safety and craft quality tend to degrade before architecture and overall pace visibly fail.

When harness constraints are weak, the main limiting factor for preserving judgment and taste in agent-first workflows is the human review bottleneck—reviewer time, context, and diff size—rather than harness rules themselves.

Strong harnesses are most effective when they shape what reaches human review—diff size, risk level, and boundary scope—rather than when they try to fully encode architectural and aesthetic judgment in rules.

Small, high-context teams with selective merge practices can often sustain better safety and craft with relatively light harnessing than large, low-context teams can achieve even with strong harness rules.

assumptions: Agents can already produce plausible, compiling code across much of the codebase (implementation abundance).
Tests and monitoring exist but are incomplete and not fully reliable as sole safety nets.
Human reviewers are under moderate time pressure and cannot deeply analyze every large diff.
Teams keep using agents heavily even after some incidents, rather than sharply reducing usage.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: With modern models and decent existing tests, removing most harness constraints would mainly increase pace and leave safety, craft, and architecture largely intact; incidents would be rare enough that stronger harnesses are optional rather than central to preserving judgment and taste.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In a very small, very high-context team with strong tests and high review discipline, removing most harness constraints might not noticeably degrade safety or craft for a long time, leading that team to conclude harness strength is unnecessary and overstating the general risks described here.
verification_targets: Sample incident and defect patterns from one or two teams that experimented with weaker harness rules and compare pre/post rates of subtle regressions and cleanup PRs.
Measure review time per LOC and diff size distributions before and after relaxing harness constraints to see whether reviewers are asked to judge larger, lower-signal diffs.
Interview reviewers about how often they felt unable to fully assess agent-generated diffs after harness constraints were reduced, and correlate with observed taste or architecture regressions.
open_questions: What is the minimum harness surface—e.g., diff size caps, boundary tags, or task-type flags—that most improves review quality without materially slowing teams down?
How does the failure pattern differ between monoliths with strong tests and multi-service systems with weaker end-to-end coverage when harness constraints are relaxed?
Can lightweight, reviewer-facing tools (e.g., auto-summarized risk hotspots in a diff) partially substitute for strict harness constraints without losing craft?

question.text: In small, implementation-abundant monolith teams that already use boundary tags and change-type flags, what additional, empirically-checkable signals inside the harness (e.g., repeated cross-boundary tool call patterns, rising counts of new helpers, growing test blast radius) best predict that architectural erosion is starting months before humans feel it in reviews or incidents?

artifacts.answer:
Most useful early-warning signals are simple, countable patterns the harness can log and trend by boundary and change-type:

1) Cross-boundary usage drift
- Rising rate of cross-boundary edits per feature/bugfix (normalized by total edits).
- Increasing distinct boundary pairs touched in a single PR or task.
- Growth in “fan-out” per boundary: more unique other boundaries referenced from one.

2) Helper and API proliferation
- Net new helpers/services per boundary per month, especially small one-off files.
- Rising number of call sites per generic helper (e.g., `Utils`, `CommonHelpers`).
- Multiple new entrypoints for the same capability (e.g., more than N ways to send email or fetch a user).

3) Test blast radius and coupling
- Median and p90 test files touched per PR for “local” change-types drifting up.
- More tests whose setup crosses 3+ boundaries for behavior that was previously local.
- Increased share of new tests that touch global fixtures or integration layers.

4) Boundary integrity violations
- More changes where files move across boundaries without a matching refactor/change-type flag.
- New files created in protected or “core” dirs without corresponding change-type justification.
- Growing number of cyclical dependency paths between boundaries (can be approximated via import/constant reference graphs).

5) Churn and revert patterns
- High churn (repeated edits) on the same boundary seams or shared helpers over short windows.
- Rising rate of cross-boundary changes that get partially reverted or followed by hotfixes.

For early detection, teams can:
- Log these metrics per boundary and change-type in the harness.
- Track simple thresholds and slopes (month-over-month deltas) rather than absolute numbers.
- Treat sustained uptrends in 2–3 of these signals as a trigger for lightweight boundary reviews or targeted refactors before humans feel pain in incidents or reviews.

claims.text: In small, implementation-abundant monolith teams, early architectural erosion shows up first as shifts in simple structural metrics—cross-boundary edit rates, helper proliferation, and widening test blast radius—before it appears in human reviews or incidents.
Harness-level logging of cross-boundary changes, new helper counts, and test touch patterns per boundary and change-type can provide practical, empirically-checkable early-warning signals of erosion.
Sustained increases over time in multiple erosion indicators are more predictive of real architectural drift than any single raw count or one-off spike.

assumptions: The codebase is a monolith with reasonably stable, tagged boundaries.
The harness can already classify change-types and attribute edits to boundaries.
Teams are willing to add lightweight logging and dashboards for structural metrics.
Architectural erosion correlates with observable structural drift in code and tests.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Most meaningful architectural erosion is driven by a few large, deliberate boundary changes that are already visible in reviews, so fine-grained harness metrics add noise and overhead without materially improving early detection.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very dynamic or poorly tagged codebases, normal evolution (e.g., planned consolidations or new domains) may produce similar metric shifts, leading to false alarms and metric fatigue; teams may ignore the signals or overcorrect and block useful change.
verification_targets: Instrument one team’s harness with the proposed metrics and compare trends to architect retrospectives about when erosion was first noticed.
Retrospectively analyze a past 6–12 month window around a known architecture cleanup to see which signals moved earliest and most strongly.
Run a small trial where sustained multi-signal drift triggers a targeted boundary review and measure whether reviewers find real issues earlier.
open_questions: Which small subset of metrics offers the best signal-to-noise ratio for most monoliths?
How should thresholds and trends be normalized for team size, feature velocity, and planned large refactors?
Can agents themselves help summarize and explain metric drift in human-reviewable form without encouraging overreaction to noise?

question.text: If we stop treating the *agent harness* as an engineering tool and instead model it as a shared ‘team contract surface’—on par with interface contracts and test narratives—what breaks in current assumptions about who owns production safety and taste, and how might that shift reveal new failure modes (e.g., prompt-layer architecture drift, UX-driven overexposure of tools) or new leverage (e.g., cross-role ownership of verification, designer-authored guardrails) that existing engineer-centric workflows systematically miss?

artifacts.answer:
Core shift: the harness stops being “engineering plumbing” and becomes a shared contract like an API or test spec. That moves both safety and taste from individual engineers toward cross-role ownership.

What assumptions break
1) "Engineers own safety"
- Break: prompts, tool exposure, and auto-verifiers encode much of runtime risk.
- New reality: PMs/designers can introduce or widen risk via harness changes (new tools, weaker checks) even if core code is stable.

2) "Taste lives in code style"
- Break: most visible taste is now in harness flows: which tools exist, how they’re named, default paths, and verification narratives.
- New reality: design and product choices shape code aesthetics indirectly (what agents tend to emit and how they’re constrained).

3) "Review = code review"
- Break: many impactful changes happen in prompts, tool wiring, and verification scripts outside normal PR habits.
- New reality: the review bottleneck sits at the harness layer; unreviewed harness edits can reconfigure large swaths of behavior.

4) "Harness = infra, not product"
- Break: changes to harness shape UX and ops just like API contracts do.
- New reality: harness changes must be versioned, reviewed, and tested as first-class product artifacts.

New failure modes
1) Prompt-layer architecture drift
- Harness prompts and examples encourage cross-boundary access (“just call whatever you need”), bypassing code-level boundaries.
- Drift is invisible in traditional architecture docs because it lives in prompt and tool usage patterns.

2) UX-driven overexposure of tools
- To unblock use cases, non-engineers expose powerful tools (e.g., prod DB writes, cross-system deletes) with weak or generic guards.
- You get incidents whose root cause is “tool should never have been accessible there,” not a bad line of code.

3) Contract skew between code and harness
- Code evolves; harness prompts, examples, and verifiers lag behind.
- Agents act on outdated narratives of invariants and error modes, causing subtle logic bugs despite green tests.

4) Invisible policy changes
- A small harness tweak (e.g., changing a verification threshold or allowed action list) effectively changes policy.
- Without explicit review and logging, teams ship “policy by prompt edit.”

New leverage
1) Cross-role verification ownership
- Designers/PMs write scenario-style checks; QA encodes regression narratives; engineers supply low-level invariants.
- Safety becomes a shared asset: more perspectives on “what must never happen.”

2) Designer-authored guardrails
- Designers encode UX and consent rules directly in harness flows (multi-step confirmations, previews, dry-runs by default).
- Safer defaults without waiting on bespoke engineering every time.

3) Taste encoded as harness patterns
- Teams capture “good taste” as:
  - preferred tool sequences,
  - standard test narratives,
  - canonical examples in prompts.
- Agents emit code closer to the craft bar by default; humans curate patterns instead of hand-fixing every diff.

4) Shared ownership of ambition
- Anyone who can edit harness flows can cheaply test new cross-system workflows.
- Seniors shift from hand-building each spike to defining safety envelopes and deciding which experiments graduate into supported flows.

Practical adjustments
- Treat harness assets (prompts, tool configs, verification scripts) as code:
  - live in VCS, go through PRs, have owners.
- Add harness-focused review checks:
  - "Does this change expose new capabilities? To whom?"
  - "Does it bypass existing boundaries or tests?"
- Add simple observability:
  - log which tools and flows are used, by which agents, under which prompts.
- Run periodic “harness sweeps” like architecture sweeps:
  - look for tool overexposure, cross-boundary prompts, and stale narratives.

Net: repositioning the harness as a contract surface forces teams to admit that safety and taste now live partly in a multi-role, prompt-and-tool layer. That creates new ways to fail and new levers for shared verification and better default taste that engineer-only workflows tend to miss.

definitions.name: agent-first workflow
definitions.meaning: A development loop where the first serious draft, analysis, or implementation attempt is delegated to an agent before the human codes directly.

definitions.name: agent harness
definitions.meaning: A tool environment that lets a model act with files, shell commands, diffs, and other capabilities instead of only producing chat responses.

definitions.name: craft bar
definitions.meaning: The explicit or implicit standard a team uses for elegance, coherence, maintainability, and fit in shipped code.

definitions.name: review bottleneck
definitions.meaning: The point where human validation, not code generation, becomes the main constraint on shipping safely.

definitions.name: ambition frontier
definitions.meaning: The boundary of projects, fixes, and optimizations a team considers worth attempting at current cost and effort levels.

definitions.name: implementation abundance
definitions.meaning: A condition where generating plausible code is cheap relative to deciding what to build and how to verify it.

claims.text: Treating the agent harness as a shared team contract surface shifts de facto ownership of production safety from engineers alone to a multi-role layer of prompts, tool exposure, and verification scripts.
When harness assets are not treated as first-class reviewed artifacts, new failure modes emerge, including prompt-layer architecture drift, UX-driven overexposure of tools, and silent policy changes.
Modeling the harness as a contract surface enables new leverage, such as cross-role ownership of verification and designer-authored guardrails that better align automation with UX and safety goals.

Engineer-centric workflows systematically under-detect harness-layer risks and underuse harness-layer leverage because they focus review and ownership on core code rather than prompts, tools, and verification flows.

assumptions: Teams are already in implementation abundance, with agents handling a meaningful share of first-pass changes.
Non-engineering roles either already touch or plausibly could touch harness assets like prompts and tool configs.
Harness changes can materially affect system behavior without corresponding core code changes.
Organizations are willing to treat harness artifacts as versioned, reviewable assets rather than ephemeral configuration.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Even if the harness is framed as a shared contract surface, engineers will retain practical ownership of safety and taste because most impactful risks and aesthetics still arise in core code and infra, making harness governance a secondary concern rather than a central craft surface.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In low-maturity or highly regulated environments, attempts to broaden harness ownership may either be blocked outright or devolve into uncontrolled edits without adequate review, increasing risk without realizing the intended cross-role leverage.
verification_targets: Audit a team’s last few incidents to classify how many originated from harness-layer changes versus core code changes.
Introduce basic version control and PR review for harness assets and observe whether incident patterns or review focus shift over several sprints.
Instrument harness usage to log tool calls and cross-boundary actions, then check for previously invisible architecture drift or overexposed tools.
open_questions: What is the minimum viable review and ownership model for harness assets that meaningfully reduces risk without creating heavy process?
How should teams partition which roles can change which parts of the harness (tools vs prompts vs verifiers) to balance leverage and safety?
Can agents reliably assist in auditing harness assets for boundary violations and overexposed tools without introducing new blind spots?

question.text: In small, agent-first monolith teams that already use boundary tags, erosion metrics, and triad pairing, where do we still see the review bottleneck producing the most value—spotting architectural drift, catching verification gaps, or correcting weak product framing—and how could we reshape PR templates, checklists, or harness prompts so that scarce senior review time is allocated explicitly to that one highest‑leverage layer?

artifacts.answer:
In these teams, the review bottleneck usually yields the most value in catching *verification gaps*, with architectural drift second and product framing third.

Reasoning (short):
- Boundary tags + erosion metrics already constrain most silent architectural drift and surface it via metrics/Arch Drift loops.
- Triad pairing and learning PRs already expose many framing errors earlier, during shaping and implementation.
- What still routinely slips is: missing or weak tests, unclear acceptance criteria, and unverified edge cases for agent-written code.

Concrete reshaping so seniors focus on verification:

1) PR template (3 required, 1 optional block)
- Problem & intent (author): one or two sentences max.
- Behavior & risk: bullet list of changed behaviors and main risk.
- Verification plan & evidence (mandatory):
  - "How verified?" checklist (e.g., unit tests, contract tests, manual steps, sandbox runs).
  - Links to test files or harness runs.
- Optional: "Architectural change" flag (Y/N). If Y, auto-tag a separate arch-focused reviewer but keep that lane light.

2) Review checklist (for all reviewers, but tuned for seniors)
- Mandatory verification questions:
  1) "Do tests cover the main behavior and at least one edge case?" (Y/N)
  2) "Is the verification story clear enough that you’d trust a rollback-less deploy?" (Y/N)
  3) "Is any risk left untested that should be called out explicitly?" (short comment)
- Architecture as a fast gate, not the main focus:
  - Single checkbox: "Any boundary violation or new pattern that needs an Arch Drift ticket?" (Y/N). If Y, harness opens/links one.

3) Harness prompt defaults
- When agents propose changes in tagged boundaries:
  - Prepend a short, fixed prompt: "Always propose tests or checks you expect a human to add. Prefer editing existing tests in the same boundary."
  - Ask the agent to output a `VERIFICATION_NOTES` block (bullets), which is pasted directly into the PR template.
- For risky change types (schema, money, auth):
  - Require the agent to emit a minimal change/rollback plan, which reviewers scan mainly for verification gaps.

4) Routing senior review to verification
- Default rule: seniors are auto-requested only when:
  - `verification_required = high` (set by checklist or change-type flag), or
  - the PR touches high-risk areas (money, auth, data retention).
- For medium/low-risk PRs with strong verification notes and green checks, any emerging crafter-level reviewer can approve.

Net effect: boundary tags + erosion metrics keep arch drift mostly in check; triads catch many framing issues; PR and harness changes explicitly pull scarce senior attention toward designing, judging, and hardening verification, where their judgment has the highest marginal impact in an implementation-abundant monolith.

claims.text: In small, agent-first monolith teams with boundary tags, erosion metrics, and triad pairing, the marginal value of scarce senior review time concentrates most in catching verification gaps rather than in spotting architectural drift or correcting product framing.

Aligning PR templates, review checklists, and harness prompts around explicit verification plans and evidence can shift the primary review bottleneck toward verification design and away from line-by-line implementation review.
Using lightweight architectural flags and erosion-driven Arch Drift tickets allows architecture concerns to stay visible without consuming most senior review energy on ordinary PRs.

Having agents emit structured verification notes that flow directly into PRs helps humans focus review on validating and tightening the verification story instead of rediscovering what to test from scratch.

assumptions: Boundary tags and erosion metrics are reasonably tuned so most significant architectural drift is surfaced outside of ad-hoc PR review.
Triad pairing is functioning such that major product framing mistakes are often caught during shaping or live collaboration, not only at merge time.
Tests and other verification artifacts are currently uneven in quality, so many regressions and incidents trace back to missing or weak verification rather than to pure architectural errors.
Agents are already used to generate substantial portions of code, making implementation relatively cheap compared to designing and judging verification.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: In these teams, the highest-leverage use of senior review time remains spotting architectural drift and shaping product intent, because verification gaps can be handled largely by harness-enforced rules and standardized test patterns rather than by concentrated human judgment.
artifacts.main_failure_case: If boundary tags, erosion metrics, or triad practices are weak or inconsistently applied, architectural drift and product framing errors may still dominate incident causes; in that case, over-optimizing review toward verification could let more fundamental design and boundary problems slip through.
verification_targets: Sample recent incidents and near-misses to categorize whether they stemmed mainly from verification gaps, architectural drift, or framing mistakes in similar teams.
Run a pilot where PR templates and checklists are changed as described and measure changes in regression rate and senior review time spent per PR type over 1–2 quarters.
Survey seniors and reviewers before and after the pilot about where their review time feels most valuable and which types of issues they catch most often.
open_questions: In practice, what fraction of high-severity issues in agent-first monolith teams with these guardrails still trace back to architecture and framing rather than verification gaps?
How minimal can verification-focused PR templates and checklists be while still reliably shifting review attention without creating checkbox fatigue?
To what extent can agents safely propose or even auto-generate verification plans without leading humans to over-trust those plans?

question.text: In small, opinionated monolith teams that already use boundary tags, drift metrics, and diff-bounded agent harnesses, where do human reviewers still add the most irreplaceable value—spotting cross-ticket coupling, re-scoping ambiguous work, reshaping interfaces, or something else—and how could we redesign PR templates and harness metadata so those scarce judgment calls are systematically surfaced instead of buried in generic review noise?

artifacts.answer:
Humans add most unique value in a few judgment-heavy spots; PR and harness metadata should be designed to route attention there explicitly.

Where humans are irreplaceable
1) Cross-ticket / cross-epoch coupling
- Seeing that a “local” change secretly interacts with:
  - adjacent tickets in the same cycle, or
  - older flows / teams with different invariants.
- Typical signatures: same domain terms touched in multiple tickets, repeated edits to the same boundary in one cycle.

2) Re-scoping and reframing work
- Noticing that the current ticket shape is wrong:
  - should be split/merged,
  - should move boundaries instead of patching around them,
  - or should be killed/deflected to a cheaper path.

3) Interface shaping
- Adjusting public surfaces (methods, events, CLI verbs) so they:
  - express real domain language,
  - keep responsibilities clean,
  - stay stable for future features.

4) Verification and risk calls
- Deciding:
  - which behaviors truly matter,
  - which tests are missing or overfitted,
  - when to demand a simpler design over a clever one.

5) Taste and long-term coherence
- Naming, patterns, and boundaries that “feel wrong” despite passing tests and metrics.

How to surface these in PR templates
1) Make the PR about intent and coupling, not just diff
Add required, short fields (checkboxes + 1–2 lines each):
- Change type (single select): bugfix / feature / local refactor / cross-boundary / interface change.
- Coupling impact:
  - "Touches same concepts as ticket(s): ____" (IDs optional).
  - "Other flows likely affected: [free text, 1 line]."
- Scope sanity:
  - "Did scope grow while implementing? Y/N. If Y, what changed?" 
- Interface surface:
  - "New/changed public surface? Y/N. If Y, where and why is this the right shape?" 
- Risk & verification:
  - "What could go wrong that tests won’t easily catch?" (1–2 bullets max).
  - "Manual check steps you actually ran:" (short list).
- Taste / future use:
  - "Is this likely to be copied as a pattern? If so, what is the rule-of-thumb for reuse?"

2) Add routing tags for reviewers
- Auto-derive PR labels from template answers + harness metadata:
  - `needs-interface-judgment` if interface change + cross-boundary.
  - `needs-rescope-eye` if scope grew or multiple domains touched.
  - `coupling-hotspot` if same boundary touched by several tickets in the last N days.
- Review queues group by these tags so seniors can batch-read the 10–20% of PRs where taste/architecture matter most.

Harness metadata to support this
1) Boundary and ticket metadata
- For each agent task, require:
  - `ticket_id` and `intended_boundaries` (list).
- Harness records:
  - `actual_boundaries_touched` from diff.
  - recent tickets touching those boundaries.
- Expose in PR:
  - "Also touched by tickets: [list]." 
  - Drift score snippet (from existing metrics) for each boundary.

2) Interface and call-graph deltas
- Harness marks when diffs:
  - add/modify public methods, events, or CLI commands;
  - add new cross-boundary calls.
- Emit small summary block into PR description:
  - `public_surface_changes: [list]`
  - `new_cross_calls: from X → Y (count)`

3) Scope and churn signals
- Harness tracks for the branch:
  - number of files and boundaries added over time;
  - re-opened agent runs due to failing tests or rewrites.
- Surface as a compact line:
  - `scope_growth: low/med/high` (based on files/dirs over time),
  - `churn: N agent rewrites / test-fail cycles`.
- Auto-tag `needs-rescope-eye` when scope_growth or churn exceeds thresholds.

4) Verification metadata
- Harness attaches to PR:
  - tests added/changed per boundary;
  - which higher-level flows were exercised (smoke scripts, e2e, scenario tests).
- Show as checkboxes in PR:
  - `critical_flows_exercised=[…]` (derived, human can amend).
- If core flows or high-drift boundaries lack new/updated tests, tag `verification-gap`.

Minimal reviewer workflow
- Reviewer sees at top of PR:
  - badges: `needs-interface-judgment`, `coupling-hotspot`, `verification-gap`, `needs-rescope-eye`.
  - short auto-generated sections: coupling summary, surface changes, scope/verification hints.
- Review order:
  1) Is this the right problem and scope? (rescope badge + scope_growth).
  2) Are interfaces and boundaries right? (interface + cross-call deltas).
  3) Are couplings sane with nearby tickets and flows? (ticket/boundary list).
  4) Is verification aligned with real risk? (verification-gap + test summary).
  5) Only then skim code for local clarity and style.

This keeps human review focused on the few calls agents can’t make well: problem/shape/coupling/verification judgment, not line-by-line policing.

claims.text: In small, opinionated monolith teams with boundary tags, drift metrics, and diff-bounded agent harnesses, human reviewers add the most irreplaceable value in cross-ticket coupling detection, work re-scoping, interface shaping, verification design, and taste-level coherence, rather than in raw defect finding.

Structuring PR templates around change type, coupling impact, scope changes, interface surfaces, and risk/verification forces authors to expose the judgment-heavy aspects of a change and makes it easier for reviewers to focus on those areas.

Enriching harness metadata with boundary touch maps, interface and call-graph deltas, scope and churn signals, and verification coverage enables automatic routing tags that surface high-judgment PRs and reduce generic review noise.

A badge-based review queue that highlights PRs needing interface judgment, re-scoping, coupling review, or verification scrutiny is more effective for small agent-first teams than treating all PRs as equal in the review bottleneck.

assumptions: Boundary tags and drift metrics are already reasonably accurate and maintained.
Tests and harness checks exist but do not fully capture cross-ticket coupling and interface quality.
Reviewers have enough context to act on coupling and scope signals if surfaced clearly.
Teams are willing to adjust PR templates and harness behavior without large process overhead.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: The main remaining value of human reviewers in such teams is still local defect detection, subtle logic checking, and security/privacy review, so investing heavily in metadata and templates to surface cross-ticket coupling, scope, and interface issues yields marginal benefit compared to improving automated tests and static analysis.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In codebases with noisy boundaries, frequent emergencies, or weak adoption of templates, the added metadata and badges may become clutter that reviewers ignore, leaving high-judgment issues still discovered only via ad-hoc senior intuition.
verification_targets: Run an A/B experiment where one team uses the proposed PR template and harness tags and a similar team does not, then compare frequency of late-discovered coupling/interface issues and review time spent per PR.
Log which PRs reviewers subjectively rate as "high-judgment" before and after introducing badges and metadata, and test whether the tagging scheme actually captures most of them.
Measure whether review queues become shorter and more skewed toward high-judgment PRs for seniors once badge-based routing is enabled.
open_questions: What is the smallest set of PR fields and harness-derived tags that captures most high-judgment cases without creating template fatigue?
Can agents reliably pre-fill coupling and interface summaries with low enough false-positive rates that humans still trust and use them?
How should this scheme adapt when designers or PMs also review PRs for product-level interface and scope concerns?

question.text: For agent-first teams running triad pairing and learning PRs, how could we turn the existing understanding signals (self-authored rationales, non-trivial corrections to agent output, boundary-aware test edits) into a simple, two- or three-tier "craft maturity" ladder that directly drives promotion, staffing on high-ambition bets, and on-call ownership, rather than remaining an informal reviewer gut feel?

artifacts.answer:
Proposed 3-tier craft maturity ladder using existing signals.

Tier 1 – Operator
- Signals
  - PRs: rationales mostly restate ticket/agent; few design notes.
  - Edits: agent output largely intact; human edits are small/fixups.
  - Tests: mostly agent-generated; occasional wrong boundary.
  - Review: replies are confirm/fix, little tradeoff language.
- Uses
  - Promotion: IC1–IC2; expectations about speed + safe operation.
  - Staffing: low-risk work; join ambition bets as implementer only.
  - On-call: paired/on-shadow; no solo ownership of core paths.

Tier 2 – Emerging Crafter
- Signals
  - PRs: concise, self-authored rationales; references boundaries/owners.
  - Edits: regular simplifications, renames, boundary moves vs. agent.
  - Tests: adjusts/creates tests in correct boundary; removes over-wide tests.
  - Review: asks clarifying questions; sometimes proposes alternatives.
- Simple rubric (per PR or sample set)
  - >=60–70% PRs with non-trivial manual edits.
  - >=60–70% PRs with clear rationale that mentions domain/boundary.
  - Few repeated review comments on the same boundary mistake.
- Uses
  - Promotion: IC2→IC3; shows system understanding.
  - Staffing: can co-own medium-ambition bets with a senior.
  - On-call: primary for well-bounded services; backup for core.

Tier 3 – System Crafter
- Signals
  - PRs: short design notes; calls out tradeoffs and future cleanups.
  - Edits: restructures agent output for clarity; introduces small patterns.
  - Tests: reshapes suites to better reflect boundaries and failure modes.
  - Review: spots agent shortcuts; gives taste feedback to others.
  - Meta: initiates learning PRs (e.g., refactors, boundary fixes) without prompts.
- Simple rubric
  - High share of PRs where:
    - agent code is significantly reorganized or simplified; and
    - tests clearly document the intended contract of a boundary.
  - Peers request them on tricky reviews and incident postmortems.
- Uses
  - Promotion: IC3+; staff/principal track.
  - Staffing: lead high-ambition bets; shape harness/patterns.
  - On-call: owns core domains; designs runbooks and guardrails.

How to make this operational (not gut feel)
1) Lightweight PR tags
- Reviewer or author tags PR after review: `T1-operator`, `T2-emerging`, `T3-system`.
- Guidance popover: 1–2 bullet criteria per tier.
- Aggregate per-person over last N PRs; show in perf/promo packets.

2) Simple scoring sheet for calibration
- Once/quarter, each senior rates a small PR sample per junior:
  - Rationale: 0 = none/boilerplate, 1 = local, 2 = boundary/system.
  - Corrections: 0 = trivial, 1 = local cleanup, 2 = structural change.
  - Tests: 0 = none/wrong, 1 = basic, 2 = boundary-aware/contract.
- Sum over 5–10 PRs to place engineer mostly in T1/T2/T3.

3) Direct links to staffing and promotion
- Promotion docs
  - Require recent examples mapped to ladder: 2–3 PRs each showing the tier behaviors.
- Ambition bets
  - Rule of thumb: leads must be T3; core implementers at least T2.
- On-call
  - Solo primary for core components requires sustained T2+ signals.

4) Keep ladder separate from raw throughput
- Track throughput and tier separately; don’t average.
- Make it explicit that promotions above IC2 require Tier 2+ craft maturity regardless of volume.

This keeps the ladder simple (3 tiers, a few visible signals) and tightly wired to actual decisions (promo, bets, on-call) instead of vague reviewer sentiment.

definitions.name: agent-first workflow
definitions.meaning: A development loop where the first serious draft, analysis, or implementation attempt is delegated to an agent before the human codes directly.

definitions.name: agent harness
definitions.meaning: A tool environment that lets a model act with files, shell commands, diffs, and other capabilities instead of only producing chat responses.

definitions.name: craft bar
definitions.meaning: The explicit or implicit standard a team uses for elegance, coherence, maintainability, and fit in shipped code.

claims.text: A three-tier craft maturity ladder based on self-authored rationales, non-trivial corrections to agent output, and boundary-aware test edits can be made operational with lightweight PR tagging and periodic structured sampling.

Using craft maturity tiers as explicit gates for promotion, staffing on high-ambition bets, and on-call ownership aligns incentives toward understanding and taste rather than raw agent-mediated throughput.
Separating craft maturity signals from throughput metrics reduces the risk that agent-first teams over-reward high-volume operator behavior at the expense of long-term system understanding.

assumptions: Teams already use agent-first workflows with triad pairing and learning PRs.
Reviewers are willing to add minimal tags or scores during normal review.
Existing signals like rationales, edits, and tests correlate with real understanding and on-call performance.
The organization is small or high-context enough that a 3-tier ladder can be calibrated across teams.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Informal reviewer judgment plus traditional performance reviews are sufficient to assess craft in agent-first teams, and adding an explicit ladder and tagging system mainly adds bureaucracy without improving promotion or staffing outcomes.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very busy or low-discipline teams, PR tagging and sampling are applied inconsistently or gamed; engineers optimize for visible signals (e.g., verbose rationales) without deeper understanding, so tiers lose credibility and staffing decisions quietly revert to gut feel.
verification_targets: Correlate craft tier scores with on-call performance and incident quality over 6–12 months.
Compare promotion and staffing decisions before vs. after ladder adoption to see if high-throughput but low-craft engineers are treated differently.
Sample PRs across tiers to check inter-reviewer agreement on tier assignments and refine criteria if variance is high.
open_questions: What is the smallest viable set of signals and thresholds that still yields stable tier distinctions across reviewers?
Can parts of the tagging and sampling process be automated from diffs and comments without encouraging gaming?
How often should tiers be recomputed or reviewed to reflect growth without creating churn or label fatigue?

question.text: For high-context teams where agents handle most first-pass diffs and seniors budget time across ambition bets, craft refactors, and apprenticeship, what is the smallest practical set of calendar and WIP constraints (e.g., protected refactor blocks, per-cycle learning-PR quotas, caps on concurrent ambition spikes) that reliably prevents review bottlenecks and overwork spirals without materially reducing shipped features?

artifacts.answer:
Proposed minimal constraints for such teams:

1) Senior review WIP cap
- Per senior: max 2 in-flight PRs that require deep review (ambition or risky refactor).
- Harness/PM tool enforces: new high-risk work can’t start if reviewer at cap.

2) Fixed weekly “craft/refactor” block
- Per senior: 1 half-day / week, calendar-protected.
- Used only for: structural refactors, test hardening, or follow-through on drift hotspots.
- No new feature work or meetings in this block.

3) Per-cycle ambition spike limit
- Per team per 1–2 week cycle: max 1–2 named “ambition spikes” (large bets / novel flows).
- Each spike must name a primary senior reviewer and explicit acceptance/rollback checks before work starts.

4) Learning-PR quota for juniors
- Per junior per cycle: 1–2 small, clearly-scoped learning PRs (agent-assisted allowed) with explicit learning goal.
- These PRs are prioritized in senior review queues but must be size-limited to avoid blocking.

5) Daily review buffer
- Per engineer: last 60–90 minutes of day reserved for review, verification, and cleanup; no new tickets started.

These five constraints aim to keep review load bounded, ensure some refactor/craft time, and preserve apprenticeship, while leaving most of the calendar open for shipping features and using agents for throughput.

claims.text: In high-context, agent-first teams, a small set of explicit WIP and calendar constraints can bound review load and overwork without materially reducing feature throughput.
Capping high-risk PRs per senior reviewer and limiting concurrent ambition spikes are effective ways to prevent review bottlenecks when agents generate most first-pass diffs.
Protected weekly craft/refactor blocks and small, explicit learning PR quotas allow seniors to maintain code quality and apprenticeship without overwhelming the schedule.
A daily review buffer where no new work is started helps teams keep review queues short and avoid late-night overwork in agent-first environments.

assumptions: The team already has solid tests and harness rules so review focuses on judgment, not basic correctness.
Engineers are mostly full-time on this codebase with predictable calendars.
Agent use already makes implementation abundant; the main constraint is senior attention and review.
Work is organized into short cycles (1–2 weeks) where ambition spikes can be named and tracked.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: The primary way to avoid review bottlenecks and overwork in agent-first teams is not explicit WIP/calendar constraints but stronger automation in verification and review (e.g., multi-agent reviewers, stricter harness gates), allowing seniors to handle higher review volumes without adding time boxes or caps.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very interrupt-driven or ops-heavy teams, fixed craft blocks, review buffers, and spike caps may be constantly broken by incidents and urgent asks, leading to schedule thrash and little real protection while still constraining planned feature work.
verification_targets: Pilot these five constraints on one team for several cycles and compare review queue length, lead time, and incident rate to a similar team without them.
Track senior weekly hours and after-hours work before and after adoption to see if overwork actually decreases while feature throughput stays within variance.
Survey engineers about perceived review load, clarity of priorities, and burnout risk before and after introducing the constraints.
open_questions: What is the minimum review WIP cap and ambition-spike limit that still works across different team sizes?
Can some of these constraints be enforced softly via tooling nudges instead of hard blocks without losing their effect?
How should these patterns adapt when designers or PMs also own harnesses and can start new automation flows without engineering gatekeeping?

question.text: If we flip the usual assumption that the *review bottleneck* is a problem to be minimized, and instead treat it as a scarce, allocatable resource—closer to cash or SRE error budgets—how would that change which work gets agent-first treatment, how we expose CLI/MCP surfaces, and how we set the craft bar for low- versus high-ambition bets so that implementation abundance doesn’t just maximize throughput but reshapes what the organization chooses to verify at all?

artifacts.answer:
Model: treat the review bottleneck as an explicit, budgeted asset that you spend on the most leverageful verification.

1) Budget and routing
- Give each team a quarterly "review budget" (rough units: senior-review-hours or high-attention PR slots).
- Tag changes by ambition/risk: `low`, `medium`, `high`.
- Simple rules:
  - Low: must be agent-first, auto-runnable checks, thin review or batched review.
  - Medium: agent-first, full tests + scenario checks, normal review.
  - High: agent-first plus design note, senior review mandatory, may consume multiple budget units.
- Use metrics (reopens, incidents, rework) to rebalance where review budget goes next cycle.

2) What gets agent-first treatment
- Default: all low/medium work is agent-first; humans only step in for framing and merge judgment.
- Reserve human-from-scratch coding mainly for:
  - novel core patterns
  - boundary-defining modules
  - verification and harness code
- Effect: throughput work (CRUD, glue, migrations, copy changes) runs mostly as agent jobs consuming minimal review budget; senior attention concentrates on frames and verifiers.

3) CLI/MCP surface design under a review budget
- Treat every tool as having a review cost class:
  - Class A (cheap): read-only, local transforms, refactors, queries.
  - Class B (moderate): reversible writes, config, low-risk scripts.
  - Class C (expensive): money, schema, external side effects.
- Design tools so most day-to-day changes use Class A/B only, with built-in, machine-checkable guards.
- Require:
  - high-friction wrappers (change plans, approvals) for Class C tools
  - clear ownership + logs for any cross-boundary or irreversible tool
- Bias CLI/MCP UX toward producing:
  - small diffs
  - explicit intent summaries
  - auto-generated verification steps agents can run before asking for review

4) Craft bar by ambition level
- Low-ambition work (bugs, small UX tweaks, local refactors):
  - Craft bar: "human-verifiable quickly." Simple, boring code; tight, readable tests; clear logs.
  - Aim: minimize review cost per unit of surface area changed.
- High-ambition work (new flows, core design, cross-system features):
  - Craft bar: "worth careful review." Clear design note, boundaries explicit, tests express invariants and failure modes, not just happy path.
  - Allow higher local complexity if it sharply reduces future review cost (good contracts, clear modules, strong checks).
- Policy: don’t raise the low-work craft bar to match high bets; instead, raise the *verification* bar only where the payoff for spending review budget is highest.

5) How this reshapes what gets verified
- Stop trying to review everything equally; instead:
  - Verify deeply: boundary-defining contracts, high-risk flows, harness/verifier code, expensive tools.
  - Verify lightly: routine wiring, local UI, internal-only plumbing, agent-maintained tests that touch non-critical areas.
- Let some low-importance surfaces become "best-effort" (tested, but rarely line-reviewed); reclaim that attention for:
  - scenario tests describing business promises
  - cross-service contract tests
  - verification harnesses agents will reuse across many changes

6) Practical mechanisms
- Require each PR/task to carry two short tags:
  - `ambition_level: low|med|high`
  - `review_intensity: auto|light|deep` (with simple allowed combinations)
- Harness/CLI behavior by tag:
  - `auto`: agents must supply tests + runbook-like verification text; merge allowed with spot checks.
  - `light`: diff capped, tests required; reviewer focuses on intent/test quality, not every line.
  - `deep`: design note required; senior reviewer assigned; optional multi-model or tool-based verification.
- Track where review time actually goes; adjust tagging or rules when incidents cluster in supposedly "low" or "auto" lanes.

Net effect: implementation abundance mostly feeds agent-first, low-review-cost work, while the consciously budgeted review bottleneck is spent on shaping interfaces, tests, and high-ambition bets that permanently improve what and how the org can afford to verify.

claims.text: Treating the review bottleneck as a budgeted asset pushes teams to route most routine work through agent-first, low-review-cost paths and concentrate scarce human attention on interfaces, verification code, and high-ambition changes.

Classifying CLI/MCP tools and agent capabilities by review cost and risk encourages designs where most operations are cheap to review and only a small set of high-impact tools require heavy human gating.

Differentiating the craft bar by ambition level—favoring quick human-verifiability for low-ambition work and richer design and tests for high-ambition work—allows implementation abundance to expand the ambition frontier instead of merely increasing throughput.
Explicit ambition and review-intensity tags per change, backed by harness behavior and simple policies, are a practical way to align agent-first workflows with a consciously allocated review budget.

assumptions: Teams can reasonably estimate change ambition and risk when tagging work.
Agent harnesses and CLIs can enforce different behaviors based on tags and tool classes without excessive friction.
Senior reviewers are scarce enough that reallocating their attention meaningfully changes system outcomes.
Incident and rework data can be collected and used to adjust review-budget policies over time.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Treating review as a budgeted asset adds unnecessary overhead; simpler heuristics like "review all production-affecting changes thoroughly" and "use agents only for low-risk code" are sufficient, and attempts to fine-tune review allocation by ambition level and tool class create confusion and process churn without clear benefit.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In teams with weak tagging discipline or poor risk estimation, work is consistently mislabeled as low-ambition or auto-review to avoid friction; review budgets become nominal, high-risk changes slip through lightly reviewed paths, and incidents rise without clear attribution to the allocation model.
verification_targets: Run a pilot where one team classifies work by ambition and review intensity and compare incident and rework rates to a similar team using uniform review policies.
Instrument harness and CLI usage to see whether most changes can be safely handled through low-review-cost tool classes without increasing production issues.
Analyze where senior review time is spent before and after adopting a review-budget model to confirm that attention shifts toward interfaces, verification artifacts, and high-ambition changes.
open_questions: What is the simplest ambition and review-intensity taxonomy that teams can apply reliably under time pressure?
How often should review budgets and tool risk classes be recalibrated in response to incidents and drift?
Can agents help propose or validate ambition and review tags without systematically underestimating risk?

question.text: Once agents reliably handle most first-pass changes in an opinionated stack, how should teams explicitly reallocate senior time across three competing activities—(1) higher-ambition bets and hunch tests, (2) raising and enforcing the craft bar via refactors and taste reviews, and (3) apprenticeship rituals for juniors—so that none of these become a permanent “nice to have” that always loses to short-term throughput?

artifacts.answer:
Suggested split for small, high-context, agent-first teams (once first-pass changes are mostly handled by agents):

1) Make the split explicit per cycle
- Allocate senior time by budget, not vibes (per 1–2 week iteration):
  - ~35–45%: (1) ambition bets & hunch tests
  - ~30–35%: (2) craft bar & refactors
  - ~20–25%: (3) apprenticeship
- Put these as calendar blocks and WIP limits, not just intentions.

2) Wire each bucket into core rituals
- (1) Ambition
  - Every cycle: at least 1 named “bet” owned by a senior (agent-heavy build, spike, or optimization).
  - Standup: 1–2 minutes reserved for bet status and next hunch to test.
- (2) Craft
  - Reserve a fixed weekly “refactor window” (e.g., 3–4h) where seniors only do boundary refactors, test hardening, and taste reviews.
  - Use PR lanes: mark some PRs as “craft/taste” so seniors can batch-review for style/architecture, not just bugs.
- (3) Apprenticeship
  - Schedule at least one triad session (senior + junior + agent) per junior per week.
  - Require 1–2 “learning PRs” per junior per cycle with explicit learning goals and senior comments focused on reasoning, not speed.

3) Tie protection of the split to metrics and constraints
- Define simple guardrails:
  - No cycle closes without: (a) a bet outcome logged, (b) at least one scoped refactor or craft-improving change merged, (c) each junior having at least one reviewed learning PR or triad.
  - Cap senior review load per day; overflow work must be descoped or moved to the next cycle, not paid for by cutting craft/apprenticeship.
- Track 2–3 light metrics:
  - Bet throughput: bets started vs. completed.
  - Craft drift: number of “architecture/taste” review comments per PR over time.
  - Apprenticeship: triad sessions and learning PRs per junior.

4) Integrate agents so they buy time for all three
- Use agents to:
  - Prep bet spikes (scaffolds, benchmarks) so seniors focus on deciding what to pursue next.
  - Do first-pass refactors and test additions, leaving seniors to judge boundaries and naming.
  - Generate exercises, starter diffs, and explanations for juniors; seniors review understanding, not raw code.

5) Reset the allocation when pressure spikes
- When deadlines hit, allow a short-term tilt (e.g., +10–15% more on throughput/ambition) but:
  - Make the deviation explicit in planning.
  - Add a rule: after any crunch cycle, the next cycle over-weights (2) craft and (3) apprenticeship until the running average returns to the target split.

Net effect: treat ambition, craft, and apprenticeship as first-class work with explicit time budgets, calendar presence, and simple completion checks, using agents to keep those budgets intact rather than as a reason to cannibalize them for more short-term output.

claims.text: In agent-first teams where agents handle most first-pass changes, senior time must be budgeted explicitly across ambition, craft, and apprenticeship or those activities will be consistently sacrificed to short-term throughput.
Simple time budgets per iteration, wired into calendars and rituals, are an effective way to ensure seniors spend meaningful time on higher-ambition bets, craft improvements, and apprenticeship.
Using agents to prepare spikes, first-pass refactors, and junior exercises allows seniors to focus on judgment-intensive parts of ambition, craft, and teaching rather than raw implementation.

Lightweight guardrails and metrics tied to each bucket—such as requiring at least one ambition bet, one craft-improving change, and one learning PR per junior per cycle—reduce the risk that any bucket becomes a permanent nice-to-have.
Allowing explicit, time-bounded deviations from the target allocation during crunch, followed by compensating cycles, helps maintain long-run balance between throughput, craft, and apprenticeship.

assumptions: Teams have reached a state of implementation abundance where agents reliably handle most first-pass changes with an agent harness and diff-first workflows.
The team is small and high-context, so explicit time budgeting and ritual changes are socially feasible.
Leadership values long-term craft and apprenticeship at least somewhat, not only short-term feature throughput.
Basic review and testing practices already exist so that senior time is not fully consumed by production firefighting.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: As models improve, the best use of senior time will be to double down on ambition and product-level decisions, allowing agents and lightly supervised juniors to handle craft and apprenticeship implicitly without strict time budgets or rituals.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In low-maturity or highly interrupt-driven environments, seniors may be unable to protect the proposed time budgets, so urgent firefighting and feature work continually erode craft and apprenticeship time despite the plan.
verification_targets: Observe 2–3 small agent-first teams that adopt explicit senior time budgets across ambition, craft, and apprenticeship, and compare the distribution of senior hours and incident rates before vs. after.
Track for several cycles whether teams using the proposed guardrails actually maintain at least one ambition bet, one craft-improving change, and one learning PR per junior per iteration.
Survey seniors and juniors on perceived craft quality, learning, and ability to pursue ambitious work before and after adopting explicit allocations and rituals.
open_questions: What is the minimum effective percentage allocation to craft and apprenticeship that still prevents noticeable skill and quality decay in agent-first teams?
How should the target split change for larger or lower-context organizations where senior bandwidth and review bottlenecks look very different?
Can some of the tracking of bets, craft changes, and learning PRs be automated in the harness without becoming burdensome bureaucracy?

question.text: For small, high-context teams running triad sessions (senior + junior + agent) and PR lanes, what is the lightest measurable definition of “taste growth” in juniors—e.g., fewer style nits per LOC, more proactive refactor suggestions, better interface proposals—and how could those metrics be wired into existing review and standup rituals without turning craft into a gamified scorecard?

artifacts.answer:
Proposed light, non-gamified way to measure junior taste growth and wire it into existing triad + PR-lane rituals.

1) Light definition of “taste growth”
Use 3 simple, trend-based signals per junior, sampled over (say) a month:

- Metric A – Review friction per LOC
  - Inputs: review tool labels / comment tags.
  - Track % of review comments that are style/structure vs correctness.
  - Taste growth signal: style+structure share goes down or moves “upstream” (from nits to higher-level comments), while correctness stays flat or improves.

- Metric B – Proactive improvements
  - Count per PR: "author-initiated" improvements (not in ticket):
    - small refactors,
    - naming or interface cleanups,
    - test clarifications.
  - Taste growth signal: steady increase in such notes per 100 LOC or per PR, with senior reviewers marking them as "good call".

- Metric C – Interface judgment
  - On design/mini-briefs and higher-level PRs, seniors quickly rate 0–2:
    - 0 = workable but clumsy,
    - 1 = good enough,
    - 2 = clearly thoughtful / fits system.
  - Taste growth signal: distribution shifts toward 1–2 without extra senior hand-holding in triads.

Treat these as coarse trends, not scores to compare across people.

2) Wiring into PR lanes
Reuse existing lane concepts from context (learning vs throughput vs high-risk):

- Learning PR lane
  - PR template fields:
    - "What did you choose on naming/structure?" (free text)
    - "Any opportunistic improvements you made?" (checkbox + short list)
  - Reviewer tags comments with 2 labels max:
    - `STYLE/STRUCTURE`, `CORRECTNESS`.
  - Reviewer can optionally mark author-initiated changes with `GOOD CALL`.
  - Tooling (or a light script) aggregates per-junior counts monthly.

- Throughput/high-risk lanes
  - No extra fields; reviewers still tag a sample of comments.
  - For big or risky PRs, reviewer adds a quick interface rating (0–2) in the PR summary.

3) Wiring into triad sessions
Use triads as qualitative checks, not counters:

- At the end of a triad:
  - Senior asks junior: "What would you change in the agent’s output for style/structure?" and notes 1–2 examples.
  - If junior proposes something reasonable, senior later logs one `TRIAD-GOOD-CALL` tag (text note or label) tied to that session.
- Over time, count of `TRIAD-GOOD-CALL` per month is a weak but useful proxy for live, in-situ taste.

4) Wiring into standups and feedback
Keep this light and narrative, not scoreboard:

- Monthly or per-cycle 1:1s
  - Seniors look at trends in A/B/C + 2–3 example PRs.
  - Feedback style: "Your style comments dropped and your good calls increased; next month, focus on interfaces for X boundary."
- Team-level
  - In a retro every few cycles, anonymize and aggregate:
    - total `GOOD CALL` count,
    - drop in style/structure comment share.
  - Use this to adjust teaching focus (e.g., more sessions on boundaries if interface scores lag).

Avoid:
- Public leaderboards or numeric targets.
- Comparing juniors by raw counts (different PR sizes, review strictness).

Use:
- Private trends per person.
- Qualitative examples in 1:1s and promotions.

This keeps metrics as background instrumentation that informs coaching, while craft and taste remain narrative, example-based, and locally judged by seniors.

claims.text: Taste growth in juniors can be tracked lightly by trends in review friction, proactive improvements, and simple interface ratings rather than by dense, numeric scorecards.
Tagging a small number of review comments and author-initiated changes in existing PR lanes provides enough data to see taste trends without overloading reviewers.
Using these metrics mainly in private 1:1s and retros, rather than as public leaderboards, reduces the risk of gamification while still guiding coaching and apprenticeship in agent-first teams.

assumptions: Teams already use PR lanes and can slightly adjust templates and labels.
Review tools or lightweight scripts can aggregate tagged comments and labels with low overhead.
Seniors are willing to add a small amount of structured tagging if it clearly feeds into better coaching.
Juniors will respond better to narrative feedback on trends and examples than to competitive numeric ranking.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Taste growth is too context-specific and qualitative to be captured even by light metrics, so the only reliable approach is deep qualitative review and mentorship without any structured tracking.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In practice, reviewers may under-tag or tag inconsistently, producing noisy signals; managers might still turn the metrics into KPIs or comparisons, leading to gaming and harming real craft despite the intended lightness.
verification_targets: Pilot the proposed tags and ratings on one small team for 2–3 cycles and check reviewer effort, consistency, and whether clear trends emerge per junior.
Survey juniors and seniors on whether the metrics-based feedback feels helpful and non-gamified compared to purely ad-hoc feedback.
Compare changes in review comment patterns and senior subjective assessments of taste growth before and after introducing these light metrics.
open_questions: What is the minimum tagging scheme that still yields useful trends without causing reviewer fatigue?
Can some of this tagging be inferred automatically from comment text or PR metadata without explicit reviewer effort?
How should these signals feed into promotion and leveling decisions without over-weighting easily-counted behaviors over deeper design judgment?

question.text: For high-context teams that budget senior time across ambition bets, craft refactors, and apprenticeship, how can we explicitly measure whether the *craft bar* is actually rising over quarters (e.g., via drift scores, test narrative quality, interface-contract stability, or postmortem patterns), and what minimal feedback loop would let teams adjust their calendars and WIP limits when those craft signals start to decay despite healthy-looking throughput?

artifacts.answer:
Proposal: track a small craft scorecard per quarter and wire it to simple calendar/WIP rules.

1) Craft bar metrics (quarterly, per team)
- Architecture drift score
  - Inputs (from existing harness ideas): cross-boundary calls, duplicate helpers, brittle cross-boundary tests, recurring arch-related review comments.
  - Output: 0–3 label per major boundary (0 = improving, 3 = serious drift).

- Test narrative score
  - Sample e.g. 30 changed/added tests per quarter.
  - Simple rubric 0–2 per test:
    - 0: unclear intent / integration blob.
    - 1: scenario named but setup/noise heavy.
    - 2: crisp scenario name + clear assertion of behavior/invariant.
  - Average to a team-level score.

- Interface-contract stability
  - Track: count of breaking changes to public services/CLIs/events per quarter, and % with explicit contract docs + contract tests updated.
  - Derive: stability score (few breaks, well-documented) vs churny/underdocumented.

- Postmortem craft patterns
  - Tag each incident: root cause category (missing tests, unclear contract, boundary violation, agent-misuse, other).
  - Metric: share of incidents tied to craft (tests/contracts/boundaries) and whether they repeat in same area.

2) Simple aggregate signals
- Craft index (CI)
  - Example: CI = 1/3 * (1 - normalized drift) + 1/3 * normalized test score + 1/3 * normalized contract score.
  - Keep it coarse (e.g. Low / OK / High) rather than chasing precision.
- Craft risk flags
  - Flag quarter as “craft risk” if any holds:
    - >1 boundary at highest drift level.
    - Test narrative score falls vs last quarter.
    - Craft-related incidents increasing.

3) Minimal feedback loop to adjust calendars/WIP
- Trigger
  - If quarter marked “craft risk”, auto-activate next-quarter guardrails:
    - +1 fixed craft/refactor block per senior per week (e.g. +2–3h).
    - Temporary lower cap on ambition spikes (e.g. 1 instead of 2 per cycle).
    - Slightly higher learning/craft PR quota (e.g. each junior 2 learning PRs/cycle, at least one in a drifted boundary).

- Exit criteria
  - Run metrics monthly during recovery quarter.
  - When drift scores improve or stabilize and test narrative score returns to prior baseline, relax back to normal caps.

- Day-to-day loop
  - Add a tiny “craft health” line to iteration review:
    - Drift hotspots this cycle, test sample score, any craft-rooted incidents.
    - One concrete adjustment to next cycle: where seniors spend refactor block, which boundaries get extra review, which tests to clean up.

4) How this connects to existing patterns
- Reuses earlier ideas: drift metrics, refactor blocks, ambition-spike caps, learning PRs.
- Keeps the loop light: a few quarterly metrics + one auto policy when craft decays, rather than a full KPI regime.

claims.text: High-context, agent-first teams can track whether the craft bar is rising by maintaining a small quarterly scorecard combining architecture drift, test narrative quality, interface-contract stability, and craft-related incident patterns.
A coarse craft index and simple risk flags are sufficient to decide when to temporarily rebalance senior time from ambition toward refactors and apprenticeship without detailed micrometrics.

When craft signals decay despite healthy throughput, the lowest-friction corrective loop is to automatically add protected craft time, lower concurrent ambition spikes, and increase targeted learning PRs until the craft metrics recover.
Linking quarterly craft metrics to concrete calendar and WIP rules helps prevent long-run quality and skill erosion in implementation-abundant teams without materially reducing shipped features.

assumptions: Teams already run agent-first workflows with diff-bounded harnesses and can instrument basic drift and test metrics.
High-context teams can afford lightweight quarterly sampling of tests and incidents without large overhead.
Craft-related drift and incidents are visible enough in diffs, tests, and postmortems to be measured with simple heuristics.
Temporarily shifting a modest share of senior time from ambition to craft and apprenticeship will not cause unacceptable delivery slips.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Craft quality is better maintained by investing in stronger upfront architecture and heavier automated verification, making periodic human-scored craft indices and calendar/WIP adjustments unnecessary overhead.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In heavily interrupt-driven or legacy-heavy teams, metrics may always show high drift and low test quality, triggering constant "craft risk" mode; people then ignore both the metrics and the guardrails, so neither craft nor throughput improves.
verification_targets: Pilot the proposed craft scorecard and auto-guardrail loop on one high-context team for 2–3 quarters and compare craft-related incident rate and refactor backlog to a similar team without it.
